elements.sent-2411-001: This article fully characterize the residual finiteness of conjugation quandles of the Baumslag-Solitar groups.
elements.sent-2411-002: The key difference between groups and their derived conjugation quandles is that many conjugation quandles are infinitely generated even when their underlying groups are finitely generated.
elements.sent-2411-003: Notably, we prove that the conjugation quandles of Baumslag-Solitar groups are always infinitely generated.
elements.sent-2411-004: Moreover, we show that if a conjugation quandle is Hopfian, then its underlying group is also Hopfian.
elements.sent-2411-005: From this, we identity the conditions for non-Hopfian conjugation quandles of Baumslag-Solitar groups.
elements.sent-2411-006: Quandles are algebraic structures based on Reidemeister moves in knot theory.
elements.sent-2411-007: Quandles were introduced independently by Joyce~\cite{Joyce and Matveev~\cite{Matveev .}}
elements.sent-2411-008: They have been applied in constructing invariants for knots (see, for example~\cite{Elhamdadi_Nelson ) and knotted surfaces (see~\cite{Carter ), as well as in formulating set-theoretic solutions to the Yang-Baxter equation (see~\cite{Carter_Elhamdadi_Saito ).}}}
elements.sent-2411-009: The binary operations of quandles generalize the notion of conjugation relations.
elements.sent-2411-010: In Wirtinger presentation, the fundamental group of a link is described using conjugation relations among the generators.
elements.sent-2411-011: There is an algebraic structure, called a {\em conjugation quandle , where a group $G$ is equipped with the binary operation $x * y = y^{-1 xy$, forming a quandle on $G$.}}
elements.sent-2411-012: This article will examine the residual finiteness of conjugation quandles.
elements.sent-2411-013: Before discussing the residual finiteness of quandles, we recall the concept of the residual finiteness.
elements.sent-2411-014: An algebraic structure $\mathcal{A}$ is called {\em residually finite if for all $x, y \in \mathcal{A}$ with $x \neq y$, there is a finite algebraic structure $\mathcal{P}$ and a homomorphism $\psi : \mathcal{A \rightarrow \mathcal{P}$ such that $\psi(x) \neq \psi(y)$.}}
elements.sent-2411-015: The residual finiteness of groups provides an important connection between group theory and topology.
elements.sent-2411-016: A group $G$ is residually finite if and only if the profinite topology on $G$ is Hausdorff.
elements.sent-2411-017: All finitely generated 3-manifold groups, including link groups, have been shown to be residually finite (see~\cite{Hempel ).}
elements.sent-2411-018: Similar to the residual finiteness of groups, these concepts can also be applied to quandles.
elements.sent-2411-019: It was first introduced in~\cite{Bardakov_Singh_Singh , where the authors proved that conjugation quandles are residually finite if their underlying groups are residually finite.}
elements.sent-2411-020: Notably, the same authors subsequently showed that link quandles are also residually finite (see~\cite{Bardakov_Singh_Singh_2 ).}
elements.sent-2411-021: This is significant because it ensures that finite quandles can always be chosen for the coloring of links, which is a key tool for constructing link invariants.
elements.sent-2411-022: Exploring the residual finiteness of conjugation quandles builds a bridge between geometric group theory and quandle theory.
elements.sent-2411-023: In geometric group theory, there is a challenging open problem posed by Gromov~\cite{Gromov , concerning whether every hyperbolic group is residually finite.}
elements.sent-2411-024: Since conjugation quandles are residually finite if their underlying groups are residually finite (see~\cite{Bardakov_Singh_Singh ), if there exists a non-residually finite conjugation quandle whose underlying group is hyperbolic, then there exists a non-residually finite hyperbolic group.}
elements.sent-2411-025: Moreover, a common strategy is to use a less restrictive property to approach Gromov's question, specifically by utilizing the Hopf property.
elements.sent-2411-026: An algebraic structure is called {\em Hopfian if every surjective endomorphism of the structure is an automorphism.}
elements.sent-2411-027: Mal'cev~\cite{Malcev proved that every finitely generated residually finite group is Hopfian.}
elements.sent-2411-028: Since hyperbolic groups are finitely generated, researchers have examined Gromov's question through the lens of the Hopf property.
elements.sent-2411-029: This perspective raised the question of whether there exist non-Hopfian hyperbolic groups.
elements.sent-2411-030: Recently, Reinfeldt and Weidmann~\cite{Reinfeldt_Weidmann proved that all hyperbolic groups are Hopfian.}
elements.sent-2411-031: Also subsequently, Fujiwara and Sela~\cite{Fujiwara_Sela provided an alternative proof, confirming this result.}
elements.sent-2411-032: Although non-Hopfian hyperbolic groups do not exist, the Hopf property of quandles, which remains an area of limited exploration, still offers a potential approach to addressing Gromov's question.
elements.sent-2411-033: In a way analogous to Mal'cev's result, it is shown in~\cite{Bardakov_Singh_Singh that every finitely generated residually finite quandle is Hopfian.}
elements.sent-2411-034: Moreover, it is also established in~\cite{Bardakov_Singh_Singh that conjugation quandles are residually finite if their underlying groups are residually finite, and the residual finiteness is subquandle-closed.}
elements.sent-2411-036: If all hyperbolic groups are residually finite, then every finitely generated subquandle of a conjugation quandle with a hyperbolic underlying group is Hopfian.
elements.sent-2411-037: In this article, we provide a complete characterization of the residual finiteness of conjugation quandles on the Baumslag-Solitar groups as stated in Theorem~\ref{thm:Main_Theorem .}
elements.sent-2411-038: To demonstrate this, we use Dehn quandles, which are finitely generated subquandles of conjugation quandles.
elements.sent-2411-039: The reason for using Dehn quandles lies in the major difference between groups and conjugation quandles derived from them.
elements.sent-2411-040: Specifically, many conjugation quandles are infinitely generated, even if their underlying groups are finitely generated.
elements.sent-2411-041: Regarding this, we show that the conjugation quandles of Baumslag-Solitar groups are always infinitely generated.
elements.sent-2411-042: In the latter part of the paper, as a result concerning the Hopf property of conjugation quandles, we showed that if a conjugation quandle is Hopfian, then its underlying group is also Hopfian.
elements.sent-2411-043: Accordingly, the conditions for non-Hopfian conjugation quandles of Baumslag-Solitar groups can be identified.
elements.sent-2411-044: In this section, we recall necessary definitions and known results of quandles.
elements.sent-2411-045: We begin with the definition of quandles.
elements.sent-2411-046: For $x, y \in Q$, there exists a unique $z \in Q$ such that $x = z * y$.
elements.sent-2411-047: The three conditions of a quandle $(Q, *)$ directly came from the Reidemeister moves of classical knots.
elements.sent-2411-048: In axiom (ii) of Definition~\ref{def:quandles , the element $z \in Q$ such that $x = z * y$ can be expressed by $x *^{-1 y$.}}
elements.sent-2411-049: Clearly, the {\em inverse operation $*^{-1}$ also satisfies (i)-(iii), and thus the set $Q$ equipped with the binary operation $*^{-1 : Q \times Q \rightarrow Q$ is a quandle as well.}}
elements.sent-2411-050: In the remainder of the article, we will use \begin{equation}x_1 *^{\epsilon_1} x_2 *^{\epsilon_2} \cdots *^{\epsilon_{k-1}} x_k\end{equation} to denote \begin{equation}( \cdots ((x_1*^{\epsilon_1}x_2)*^{\epsilon_2}x_3)* \cdots)*^{\epsilon_{k-1}} x_k\end{equation} in a quandle, where $\epsilon_i=\pm 1$ for each $i=1, \dots, k-1$.
elements.sent-2411-051: Quandles can be derived from groups.
elements.sent-2411-052: Especially, every group can be associated with a conjugation quandle as follows.
elements.sent-2411-053: Note that even though the underlying groups of conjugation quandles are finitely generated, this does not necessarily mean that the conjugation quandles themselves are finitely generated.
elements.sent-2411-054: We will discuss this further in Section~\ref{sec:Conjugation_quandles .}
elements.sent-2411-055: On the other hand, there exist finitely generated quandles that exhibit the same operation as conjugation quandles, as detailed below.
elements.sent-2411-056: We call {\em Dehn quandle of $G$ with respect to $X$, denoted by $\mathcal{D (X^G)$, the set $X^G$ equipped with the binary operation $x * y = y^{-1 xy$ which gives a quandle structure on $\mathcal{D (X^G)$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-057: Clearly, for each $X \subseteq G$, Dehn quandle $\mathcal{D (X^G)$ is a subquandle of $\Conj(G)$.}
elements.sent-2411-058: Also, every subquandle of $\Conj(G)$ is a Dehn quandle (see~\cite[Corollary~3.9]{Dhanwani_1 ).}
elements.sent-2411-059: Moreover, if a group $G$ is generated by $X$, then $\mathcal{D (X^G)$ is generated as a quandle by $X$ (see~\cite[Proposition~3.2]{Dhanwani_1 ).}}
elements.sent-2411-060: In this article, we will examine the conjugation quandles of Baumslag-Solitar groups.
elements.sent-2411-061: Baumslag and Solitar~\cite{Baumslag_Solitar examined various groups with the presentations \begin{equation}\BS(m,n)=\langle a, b \svert b^{-1}a^mb=a^n \rangle,\end{equation} where $m, n \in \mathbb{Z \setminus \{0\}$.}}
elements.sent-2411-062: One of the most famous examples of a non-Hopfian group is the Baumslag-Solitar group $\BS(2,3)=\langle a, b \svert b^{-1 a^2b=a^3 \rangle$.}
elements.sent-2411-063: Also we can summarize their findings as follows (with a correction contributed by Meskin~\cite{Meskin ):.}
elements.sent-2411-064: The main result of this paper is a complete classification of the residual finiteness for conjugation quandles of the Baumslag-Solitar groups.
elements.sent-2411-065: A detailed proof of the following theorem will be provided in the latter part of Section~\ref{sec:Conjugation_quandles .}
elements.sent-2411-066: Let $\BS(m,n)$ be the Baumslag-Solitar group given by the presentation \begin{equation}\BS(m,n)=\langle a, b \svert b^{-1}a^mb=a^n \rangle,\end{equation} where $m, n \in \mathbb{Z \setminus \{0\}$.}
elements.sent-2411-067: Then the conjugation quandle $\Conj(\BS(m,n))$ is residually finite if and only if $|m|=1$, $|n|=1$ or $|m|=|n|$.
elements.sent-2411-068: Every finitely generated residually finite quandle is Hopfian (see~\cite[Theorem~5.7]{Bardakov_Singh_Singh ).}
elements.sent-2411-069: However, the fact that conjugation quandles are non-Hopfian does not necessarily imply that they are always non-residually finite.
elements.sent-2411-070: This is due to the fact that, although the underlying groups of conjugation quandles are finitely generated, it does not guarantee that the conjugation quandles themselves are finitely generated.
elements.sent-2411-071: In detail, we provide an example of infinitely generated conjugation quandles along with a concrete proof.
elements.sent-2411-072: Then the conjugation quandle $\Conj(\BS(m,n))$ is infinitely generated.
elements.sent-2411-073: Suppose to the contrary that $\Conj(\BS(m,n))$ is finitely generated.
elements.sent-2411-074: Let $M$ denote the number of elements in the generating set of $\Conj(\BS(m,n))$.
elements.sent-2411-075: Assume, for the sake of contradiction, that $M$ is bounded.
elements.sent-2411-076: The proof will be divided into four cases based on the values of $m$ and $n$.. {\bf Case 1.}
elements.sent-2411-077: Since switching $m$ and $n$ leads to the same reasoning, we consider the case where $|m|=1$ without loss of generality.

elements.sent-2411-135: Next, we proceed to state the following theorem concerning the Hopf property of conjugation quandles and their underlying groups.
elements.sent-2411-137: If $\Conj(G)$ is Hopfian, then the group $G$ is Hopfian.
elements.sent-2411-138: Suppose to the contrary that $G$ is non-Hopfian.
elements.sent-2411-139: Then there exists an epimorphism $\psi : G \rightarrow G$ such that $\psi$ is not injective, which implies that $\psi$ induces the quandle epimorphism $\tilde{\psi : \Conj(G) \rightarrow \Conj(G)$ which is not injective.}
elements.sent-2411-140: Hence $\Conj(G)$ is non-Hopfian, which is a contradiction.
elements.sent-2411-142: Using Theorem~\ref{thm:Hopfian_conjugation_quandle , given a non-Hopfian group, one can construct a non-Hopfian conjugation quandle whose underlying group is the given group.}

elements.sent-2411-197: Specifically, the Wirtinger presentation of the fundamental group of a link complement involves conjugation relations of the form $z=y^{-1 xy$.}

elements.sent-2411-199: Every group gives a quandle structure defined by the binary operation: $x * y = y^{-1 xy$, called a {\em conjugation quandle .}}
elements.sent-2411-200: This article examines the residual finiteness of conjugation quandles.
elements.sent-2411-201: The residual finiteness of groups provides an important connection between topology and group theory.
elements.sent-2411-202: Similar to the residual finiteness of groups, this property can also be applied to quandles.
elements.sent-2411-203: In particular, they subsequently showed that link quandles are residually finite (see~\cite{Bardakov_Singh_Singh_2 ).}
elements.sent-2411-204: The residual finiteness allows passage from infinite link quandles to finite quandles, thus making computations of invariants arising from coloring of links simpler.
elements.sent-2411-205: Since conjugation quandles are residually finite if their underlying groups are residually finite (see~\cite{Bardakov_Singh_Singh ), the existence of a non-residually finite conjugation quandle with a hyperbolic underlying group would imply the existence of a non-residually finite hyperbolic group.}
elements.sent-2411-206: Subsequently, Fujiwara and Sela~\cite{Fujiwara_Sela provided an alternative proof, confirming this result.}
elements.sent-2411-207: Moreover, it is established in~\cite{Bardakov_Singh_Singh that conjugation quandles are residually finite if their underlying groups are residually finite, and the residual finiteness is closed under taking subquandles.}
elements.sent-2411-208: Thus, one direct approach to Gromov's question is that: if all hyperbolic groups are residually finite, then every finitely generated subquandle of a conjugation quandle with a hyperbolic underlying group is Hopfian.
elements.sent-2411-209: In this article, we prove that if a conjugation quandle is Hopfian, then its underlying group is also Hopfian in Lemma~\ref{thm:Hopfian_conjugation_quandle .}
elements.sent-2411-210: We also show that the converse does not hold in Theorem~\ref{thm:non_Hopfian_Conj(G) .}

elements.sent-2411-213: Famous examples dealing with the residual finiteness and the Hopf property are the Baumslag-Solitar groups.
elements.sent-2411-214: Hence, to analyze the connection between quandles and groups through the residual finiteness and the Hopf property, we examine conjugation quandles of the Baumslag–Solitar groups.
elements.sent-2411-215: Lemma~\ref{thm:Hopfian_conjugation_quandle gives particular cases of the conjugation quandles of the Baumslag-Solitar groups which are non-Hopfian.}
elements.sent-2411-216: However, it is difficult to use the fact that every finitely generated residually finite quandle is Hopfian since we show that conjugation quandles of the Baumslag–Solitar groups are infinitely generated in Theorem~\ref{thm:infinitely_generated .}
elements.sent-2411-217: Hence, to connect the residual finiteness to the Hopf property of quandles, it is necessary to work with finitely generated quandles.
elements.sent-2411-218: For this purpose, we employ Dehn quandles as subquandles of conjugation quandles, which allows us to fully characterize the residual finiteness of conjugation quandles of the Baumslag–Solitar groups in Theorem~\ref{thm:Main_Theorem .}
elements.sent-2411-219: In this section, we recall the necessary definitions and known facts of quandles.
elements.sent-2411-220: Any set $X$ with a binary operation satisfying $x * y=x$, for all $x, y \in X$, is a quandle called a {\em trivial quandle .}
elements.sent-2411-221: Notice that the three conditions of a quandle $(Q, *)$ directly come from the Reidemeister moves of classical knot theory.
elements.sent-2411-222: Note that a Dehn quandle is finitely generated when the subset $X$ of the group $G$ is a finite set.
elements.section-2411-005: Connection between conjugation quandles and their underlying groups through the Hopf property
elements.sent-2411-223: Conjugation quandles and their underlying groups are distinct algebraic structures that nonetheless share several similarities.
elements.sent-2411-224: There are relations between the residual finiteness and the Hopf property for both groups and quandles.
elements.sent-2411-225: In~\cite{Bardakov_Singh_Singh , it was shown that every finitely generated residually finite quandle is Hopfian, and if $G$ is residually finite, then $\Conj(G)$ is residually finite.}
elements.sent-2411-226: In this section, we show that if $\Conj(G)$ is Hopfian then $G$ is Hopfian.
elements.sent-2411-227: We also prove that the converse does not hold.
elements.sent-2411-228: We start by stating the following lemma which will also be used in Section 4.
elements.sent-2411-229: If the quandle $\Conj(G)$ is Hopfian, then the group $G$ is Hopfian.
elements.sent-2411-230: Suppose in contrast that $G$ is non-Hopfian.
elements.sent-2411-231: It is natural to ask whether the converse of Lemma~\ref{thm:Hopfian_conjugation_quandle holds.}
elements.sent-2411-232: In other words, is there a Hopfian conjugation quandle $\Conj(G)$ such that its underlying group $G$ is non-Hopfian? The following theorem gives an answer to this question:.
elements.sent-2411-233: Let $G=\langle a \rangle$ be an infinite cyclic group generated by $\{a\}$.
elements.sent-2411-234: Then $\Conj(G)$ is non-Hopfian, whereas $G$ is Hopfian.
elements.sent-2411-235: To show that $\Conj(G)$ is non-Hopfian, let $\phi : \Conj(G) \rightarrow \Conj(G)$ be a quandle map defined by $1 \mapsto 1$, $a^n \mapsto a^{n-1}$ and $a^{-n \mapsto a^{-n+1}$ for every $n \in \mathbb{Z _+$.}}
elements.sent-2411-236: Since the group $G$ is abelian, $\Conj(G)$ is a trivial quandle.
elements.sent-2411-237: Hence, any map from $\Conj(G)$ to itself is a homomorphism.
elements.sent-2411-240: But since $\phi(1)=1=\phi(a)$ with $a \neq 1$ in $\Conj(G)$, $\phi$ is not injective.
elements.sent-2411-242: Theorem~\ref{thm:non_Hopfian_Conj(G) highlights a distinction between conjugation quandles and their underlying groups.}
elements.sent-2411-243: Recall that every hyperbolic group is Hopfian~\cite{Fujiwara_Sela, Reinfeldt_Weidmann .}
elements.sent-2411-244: However, since every cyclic group is hyperbolic, Theorem~\ref{thm:non_Hopfian_Conj(G) gives an example of a non-Hopfian conjugation quandle whose underlying group is hyperbolic.}
elements.sent-2411-245: Moreover, since every trivial quandle is residually finite, Theorem~\ref{thm:non_Hopfian_Conj(G) implies that there exists an infinitely generated quandle which is non-Hopfian but residually finite.}
elements.section-2411-006: The Conjugation Quandles of the Baumslag-Solitar Groups
elements.sent-2411-246: In this section, we examine conjugation quandles of the Baumslag–Solitar groups in order to highlight their similarities and to discuss some differences.
elements.sent-2411-247: One of the most famous examples of a finitely presented non-Hopfian group is the Baumslag-Solitar group $\BS(2,3)=\langle a, b \svert b^{-1 a^2b=a^3 \rangle$.}
